narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 29.50, you've worked hard to build a strong academic foundation, coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use to evaluate applications predicted a 56.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that leads to rejection—meaning your application was denied to minimize the risk of future bar failure for students like you. This prediction was driven primarily by your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the 1st decile, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile where most admitted students rank. Your undergraduate GPA, higher than the average of 3.21, played a significant counterbalancing role, strongly pulling the prediction in a positive direction toward passing the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score, though below the dataset average of 36.70, still contributed somewhat favorably compared to even lower performers. Less influential but still helpful were your middle-income family background and full-time enrollment plans, both aligning with the majority of successful students and nudging things slightly toward success. Overall, while your GPA was a bright spot above average, your low year deciles in school outweighed it, placing you below the profile of typical admitted students who balance stronger consistent rankings with solid test scores. I know this rejection stings, especially given your dedication, but it's not a reflection of your potential—it's the model's data-driven caution based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying— you've got the GPA foundation to build on, so keep pushing. We're rooting for you to turn this into a comeback story.
